design_philosophy.core_loop: Alternate control between three Vikings with unique abilities, solving environmental puzzles by leveraging each character's distinct skills to progress through levels
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Cooperative puzzle-solving under resource constraint: all three Vikings must reach the exit, but each can only take three hits before dying, requiring careful planning and execution
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Lead a ragtag team of Vikings with complementary abilities through bizarre time-displaced locations to escape an alien zoo and return home
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Rejects direct Lemmings adaptation (hundreds of tiny managed units) in favor of direct character control with larger sprites suited to console display; emphasizes that players prefer controlling protagonists directly rather than managing crowds; mandates cooperation—each Viking's unique toolset is required, making solo-character progression impossible

difficulty.lives_system: Each Viking has three health points; death of any Viking makes the level unwinnable but allows continuation with remaining characters
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Password system allows level selection and skipping (particularly in Japanese version with MOMY password + button combo); level design mandates all three Vikings reach exit, creating inherent puzzle difficulty separate from execution difficulty

progression.upgrade_system: No persistent upgrades; each level introduces new environmental challenges and enemy types that implicitly 'teach' player mastery of character abilities
progression.unlock_mechanism: 37 levels in SNES version (42 in Genesis); progression unlocked by reaching level exit with all three Vikings; level skip password system available
replayability_hooks: Password system enables non-linear level access and speedrun potential
Co-op mode on Genesis (2-3 simultaneous players) differs from single-player alternating control
Multiple platform versions with exclusive levels (Genesis has 5 additional stages)

world_building_techniques: Thematic level design: levels set in different time periods and locations (implied by 'bizarre locations' and 'various periods of time' narrative framing)
Character-driven narrative: three Vikings with distinct personalities (Erik the Swift, Baleog the Fierce, Olaf the Stout) defined by mechanical abilities
Simple plot frame: kidnapped for alien zoo, must escape and return home, confront final boss Tomator

notable_bosses: Tomator (emperor of Croutonian empire, final boss—confrontation required to return home)

faction_indicators: Three distinct Viking archetypes: Erik (speed/aggression), Baleog (ranged offense), Olaf (defense/utility)
danger_signals: Enemies that can kill Vikings in three hits
Level design traps: falls from great heights, hazard-filled passages
environmental_storytelling: Level themes tied to time periods and alien locations
Character cameos in other games suggest broader Silicon & Synapse universe (Rock n' Roll Racing, Blackthorne 32X, ClayFighter)

sources_used: tcrf (The Cutting Room Floor - Lost Vikings SNES article)
